one_liner: Relaunch Oreo to make India re-win the World Cup.
search_doc:
Relaunch Oreo to make India re-win the World Cup.
Mechanisms: sponsorship-circumvention, cultural-moment-hijack, celebrity-as-prop, superstition-as-strategy, earned-media-stunt
Idea: Relaunch Oreo in India 'for the first time ever, again', using the lucky coincidence that Oreo's 2011 debut matched India's last World Cup win, and have legend MS Dhoni stage a press conference urging the country to recreate 2011 to bring the cup home.
Insight: Is that a mere coincidence? We don't think so. That's why with another World Cup this year, we decided to launch Oreo for the first time ever, again — using the sincerest hope that India wins the World Cup again, using a live press conference featuring former Indian World Cup-winning captain MS Dhoni.

extracted.tagline: Oreo got a nation to create history by recreating history.
extracted.context: The Cricket World Cup is the Super Bowl of India. But Oreo had a problem: not a sponsor, no big media budgets, and not even legally allowed to use 'World Cup' in their advertising. But we did find one strange coincidence — Oreo launched in India in 2011, the same year India won the Cricket World Cup.
extracted.insight: Is that a mere coincidence? We don't think so. That's why with another World Cup this year, we decided to launch Oreo for the first time ever, again — using the sincerest hope that India wins the World Cup again, using a live press conference featuring former Indian World Cup-winning captain MS Dhoni.
execution: Live press conference event featuring MS Dhoni 'relaunching' Oreo for the first time again
Hijacking of the World Cup conversation without official sponsorship
Famous cricketers and celebrities posting throwback 2011 photos in their own social posts
Earned PR / news cycle coverage
press_coverage: "Hijacking the WC without sponsoring it!" — Campaign

inferred.idea: Relaunch Oreo in India 'for the first time ever, again', using the lucky coincidence that Oreo's 2011 debut matched India's last World Cup win, and have legend MS Dhoni stage a press conference urging the country to recreate 2011 to bring the cup home.
inferred.one_liner: Relaunch Oreo to make India re-win the World Cup.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Sidestepped a sponsorship blackout by relaunching a 2011 product as a national good-luck ritual; Dhoni-led PR stunt drew 2.6BN impressions, 6M day-one views and $3M earned media without paid Cup rights.

visual.style_description: Editorial case-board: MS Dhoni in cricket kit holds an Oreo pack as the hero, against a dark blue background; left column carries dense body copy in red and white callouts, right column shows news-clipping thumbnails, and a press-logo strip plus metric callouts run along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Re-launching a 12-year-old product as a national good-luck charm is a charming sponsorship-circumvention play that lets a non-sponsor own the Cricket World Cup conversation in India. The 2011 coincidence is the engine — it turns a brand-history footnote into a tradeable cultural superstition, which is what makes it memorable.

strategy.rationale: Oreo is Mondelez's global biscuit brand whose India business launched in 2011 — using that origin date as a superstitious pivot point ties the campaign directly to the brand's own equity history rather than to a borrowed cultural cause, so mission-fit is honest. The named context is verifiable: the Cricket World Cup is genuinely the dominant sporting moment in India, MS Dhoni did captain the 2011 winning side, and Oreo did debut in India that year. The cited soft spot is the explicit constraint — 'not a sponsor, no big media budgets, not even legally allowed to use World Cup' — which makes the workaround feel earned rather than opportunistic.

execution.rationale: A single staged press conference with Dhoni 'relaunching' Oreo for the first time again is the core asset, amplified by cricketer/celebrity throwback posts and an organic news-cycle loop, with no paid Cup media. Channel choice is appropriate for a sponsorship-circumvention play — earned PR plus celebrity social rather than broadcast — but the orchestration is essentially one big stunt moment rather than a tournament-long campaign architecture, which limits how much it can compound.

impact.rationale: '2.6BN impressions' and '+6MN views in 24 hours' are entrant-reported reach claims with no audit and no absolute base for what 'impression' counts as in this measurement — directional rather than business-grade. '$3MN earned media' is a useful efficiency proxy because it implies a cost-per-outcome story for a non-sponsor, but it is the standard PR-AVE valuation method which Cannes juries discount. The press wall (Times of India, Economic Times, Mint, Campaign, LBB, Brut, afaqs!) is a credible signal of national earned coverage; the missing piece is the Oreo business outcome — Indian sales lift, share movement vs Parle/Britannia, distribution gain — which a Creative Effectiveness jury expects to see for a re-launch story.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the sponsorship-circumvention is genuinely on-brand and the press footprint is real, but the impact case is reach-and-AVE rather than the sales/share evidence a Creative Effectiveness Gold demands for a product re-launch.

one_liner: Turn your back on the filter that erases your face.
search_doc:
Turn your back on the filter that erases your face.
Mechanisms: platform-jailbreak, cultural-moment-hijack, celebrity-as-prop, self-esteem-advocacy, anti-filter-protest
Idea: Dove invited hundreds of women to fight this trend by taking away its ability to do harm: by literally turning their backs on #TurnYourBack, the initiative that started as a post and quickly spread across the internet, reverted the spotlight with its powerful message at one of the most important events in the world: the Oscars. At the hands of Gisele Turner and Gabrielle Union, and triggered the digital movement to become a global campaign. The message was supported by OOH in the world's largest markets such as US, UK, ES, IT, BR, SA, BRA, turning the conversation to beauty standards and generating a critical mind in social networks.
Insight: AI beauty filters quietly redefine 'normal' by replacing every pixel of a face with a uniform ideal, eroding the self-esteem of the young women who use them most.

extracted.tagline: No filter should tell you how to look.
extracted.context: In early March, TikTok's 'Bold Glamour' filter took more than 400 million views; it surpassed the world by becoming one of the first AI-powered filters, and was flawless in its face-finishing. The filter replaces every pixel of the face with a standardised beauty image, making it a weapon against the confidence and self-esteem of the biggest filter users: little girls and young women.
extracted.idea: Dove invited hundreds of women to fight this trend by taking away its ability to do harm: by literally turning their backs on #TurnYourBack, the initiative that started as a post and quickly spread across the internet, reverted the spotlight with its powerful message at one of the most important events in the world: the Oscars. At the hands of Gisele Turner and Gabrielle Union, and triggered the digital movement to become a global campaign. The message was supported by OOH in the world's largest markets such as US, UK, ES, IT, BR, SA, BRA, turning the conversation to beauty standards and generating a critical mind in social networks.
execution: Social-media posts and TikTok content with the back of the head turned to the camera
Oscars red-carpet activation with women turning their backs
OOH placements in the US, UK, ES, IT, BR, SA, BRA
Influencer and celebrity participation
press_coverage: "Dove kickstarted a social movement." — Women's Health
"Fighting to make social media a more positive place." — Vanity Fair
"Dove has championed wider definitions of beauty." — The Wall Street Journal

inferred.insight: AI beauty filters quietly redefine 'normal' by replacing every pixel of a face with a uniform ideal, eroding the self-esteem of the young women who use them most.
inferred.one_liner: Turn your back on the filter that erases your face.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned an exploding TikTok beauty filter into a global anti-filter protest at the Oscars, securing 1B impressions, 94% positive sentiment and 110M TikTok views, and reframing Dove's Real Beauty platform for the AI era.

visual.style_description: Hero shot of a woman with natural Afro hair photographed from behind, framed inside a faux-Instagram interface that displays the #TurnYourBack lockup; flanking columns carry dense body copy, mosaics of women turning their backs, OOH photographs, and a press logo strip across the bottom.

idea.rationale: Inverting the most viral AI-beauty filter of the moment by literally turning women's backs to camera is a sharp, memetic gesture — the act and the hashtag are the same thing, so the protest is reproducible by anyone with a phone. The reframe of 'engagement' into 'refusal' is genuinely fresh inside an over-served Real Beauty platform.

strategy.rationale: Dove is Unilever's global personal-care brand whose two-decade 'Real Beauty / Self-Esteem Project' mission has consistently positioned the brand against narrowing beauty standards, so confronting TikTok's 'Bold Glamour' filter is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The insight that AI filters replace every pixel of a face with a standardised ideal — and that the heaviest users are girls and young women — is observable and corroborated by the cited 400M Bold Glamour views. The board does not cite an internal Dove self-esteem study to anchor the harm claim, which leaves the cultural fact strong but the harm-mechanism slightly under-evidenced.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs in one move from a TikTok creator post to a high-visibility Oscars red-carpet activation with Gabrielle Union and Gisele, then to OOH in seven priority markets (US, UK, ES, IT, BR, SA, BRA), so a native social gesture is amplified into the celebrity tier and the physical world. The channels are well-matched to the audience — TikTok where the filter lives, the Oscars for cultural reach, OOH for stature — and the celebrity participation closes the loop back into earned coverage.

impact.rationale: Headline numbers are large but mostly reach-and-sentiment: '+1 billion impressions' and '+110 million TikTok views' are entrant-reported and unaudited, and the '94% positive sentiment in key markets' is a percentage without a stated post-count or methodology — strong directional signals rather than business-grade outcomes. The press constellation across Time, WSJ, Vogue, Vanity Fair, Cosmopolitan, Forbes and Marie Claire is a credible signal of earned conversation at scale, and the Women's Health and Vanity Fair quotes confirm cultural-conversation lift. The real Dove business outcome a Creative Effectiveness jury would want — Self-Esteem Project enrolment, brand-equity lift, or sales/share movement — is not shown on the board, which is the soft spot.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because the gesture is exact-fit for Dove's two-decade mission and the Oscars + OOH orchestration converted a single TikTok filter moment into globally-covered cultural conversation — even if the impact case stays at reach/sentiment rather than hard brand-equity proof.

campaign: ADLaM: An Alphabet to Preserve a Culture
one_liner: Microsoft turns a spoken language into a typeable one.
search_doc:
Microsoft turns a spoken language into a typeable one.
Mechanisms: product-as-cultural-preservation, purpose-driven-product, typography-as-platform, legislative-recognition
Idea: Partner with the two brothers who invented ADLaM, a new alphabet for Fulfulde, and digitise it inside Microsoft products so a culture can finally be written, taught and read.
Insight: A spoken language without a written script cannot defend itself — turning Fulfulde into a typeable, computer-native alphabet is the most durable way to save it.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE EFFECTIVENESS/SILVER/Adlam_ An Alphabet To Preserve A Culture - Microsoft (McCann NewYork).jpg

extracted.campaign: ADLaM: An Alphabet to Preserve a Culture
extracted.tagline: An alphabet to preserve a culture
extracted.context: The Fulani people of West Africa — one of the largest ethnic groups on the continent — had no written script. Their language, Fulfulde, was at risk of disappearing along with their culture as UNESCO warns that 90% of today's spoken languages will become extinct by the end of the century.
extracted.idea: Partner with the two brothers who invented ADLaM, a new alphabet for Fulfulde, and digitise it inside Microsoft products so a culture can finally be written, taught and read.
execution: Built the ADLaM font and shipped it inside Windows and Microsoft 365
Created an ADLaM keyboard layout
Released the first ADLaM dictionary
Funded ADLaM-focused schools
Trained ADLaM students across Mali, Guinea and beyond

inferred.insight: A spoken language without a written script cannot defend itself — turning Fulfulde into a typeable, computer-native alphabet is the most durable way to save it.
inferred.one_liner: Microsoft turns a spoken language into a typeable one.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Shipped a brand-new alphabet for Fulfulde inside Windows on 1 billion computers, enrolled 30k new ADLaM students and produced the first ADLaM dictionary — turning Microsoft into custodian of a previously unwritten language.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial poster with a bold red 'ADLaM' headline and a hero photo of a young Fulani girl reading an ADLaM script book held to her face; the ADLaM character grid runs in red at the top-right, with background, idea, execution and results columns laid out beneath in dense info-graphic blocks above a press logo strip.

idea.rationale: Shipping a brand-new alphabet inside an operating system so a culture can be typed for the first time is a genuinely original product-as-message move — the work is the typeface, not a campaign about the typeface. The category-redefinition (from advertising into language infrastructure) is what makes it memorable years after launch.

strategy.rationale: Microsoft is the world's largest software company whose stated mission is to 'empower every person and every organization on the planet to achieve more', so building Fulfulde into the OS is a near-perfect literal expression of that mission rather than borrowed cultural purpose. The context is anchored by a verifiable third-party data point — the UNESCO claim that 90% of today's spoken languages will become extinct by the end of the century — and the cultural fact that the Fulani are one of the largest ethnic groups in West Africa with no written script is observable. The insight that a spoken-only language cannot defend itself in a digital-first century is the kind of plain truth a jury can corroborate without further evidence.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is unusually deep for an ad case: a font shipped inside Windows and Microsoft 365, a keyboard layout, the first ADLaM dictionary, ADLaM-focused schools, and student training across Mali and Guinea — a five-layer system rather than a single launch asset. Channels are matched to the audience by definition (the platform IS the channel for typing) and the press orchestration through CBS, CNN, NBC, AdAge, The Atlantic and UNESCO multiplies it beyond the West-African target into global tech-press awareness.

impact.rationale: '1 billion computers' is a deployment-footprint claim (Windows install base) rather than usage of ADLaM specifically — it states reach potential, not adoption. '30k new ADLaM students' and 'first ADLaM dictionary' and 'first official alphabet in Mali' are concrete, verifiable behaviour-change outcomes that are exactly the right business outcome for a cultural-preservation product, though the scale is modest against the Fulani population (~40M). The board is missing a Microsoft-equity outcome — brand-consideration lift, reputation tracker movement, employee-pride scores — which a Creative Effectiveness jury would weight heavily for a brand-purpose case at this scale; the absence keeps Impact firmly below the Gold band.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the idea is category-redefining and the strategic fit is near-perfect, but the impact case shows cultural deployment without a corresponding Microsoft-equity outcome, which is what a Creative Effectiveness Gold would require.

one_liner: The losing sponsor lets the winning country drink the beer.
search_doc:
The losing sponsor lets the winning country drink the beer.
Mechanisms: crisis-pivot, sponsorship-jiu-jitsu, real-time-marketing, free-product-distribution, earned-media-stunt
Idea: That was when we flipped the script: we decided to give away all the beer for free, and the World Cup winner would #BringHomeTheBud. With a four-year plan pivoted in 24 hours with one tweet, the real-time campaign evolved with the tournament. Social media, OOH, OOH, social. We showed up in countries overnight, spreading our message and beer around the world to take over the 2022 World Cup and become the most talked brand of the year.
Insight: When you are publicly banned from the world's biggest stage, the only way to win is to let the audience finish the story for you — let the winning nation take your unsellable beer home as a victory trophy.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE EFFECTIVENESS/SILVER/Bring Home The Bud - Budweiser (Wieden+Kennedy, Sao Paulo _ Wieden+Kennedy, New York _ Africa Creative DDB, Sao Paulo).jpg

extracted.tagline: A major crisis. A massive comeback.
extracted.context: Two days before the World Cup kick-off, Budweiser was banned from selling beer in Qatar. Our contract was broken and we were left with a warehouse with thousands of liters of Bud and a multi-million dollar loss.
extracted.idea: That was when we flipped the script: we decided to give away all the beer for free, and the World Cup winner would #BringHomeTheBud. With a four-year plan pivoted in 24 hours with one tweet, the real-time campaign evolved with the tournament. Social media, OOH, OOH, social. We showed up in countries overnight, spreading our message and beer around the world to take over the 2022 World Cup and become the most talked brand of the year.
execution: Single launch tweet pivoting strategy in 24 hours
Real-time social media reactions and content during the tournament
OOH placements appearing overnight in countries as teams advanced
Beer shipments to the winning country (Argentina) for celebration
Beer giveaways to fans
press_coverage: "Turning unsold beer into lemonade." — The New York Times

inferred.insight: When you are publicly banned from the world's biggest stage, the only way to win is to let the audience finish the story for you — let the winning nation take your unsellable beer home as a victory trophy.
inferred.one_liner: The losing sponsor lets the winning country drink the beer.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Converted a last-minute Qatar sales ban into the most-talked-about World Cup brand of 2022: a single pivot tweet, 1M+ free beers and 25BN impressions delivered $400M in earned media and #1 World Cup brand status.

visual.style_description: Cinematic hero shot of the gold World Cup trophy in a warehouse-style red-lit corridor of stacked Budweiser cases, with the bold red-and-white 'BRING HOME THE BUD' title above; right side stacks OOH and stadium photographs in a tight grid; metric callouts and press logos sit at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Turning a publicly humiliating Qatar sales ban into a tournament-long 'the winning country drinks the unsellable beer' narrative is a clean piece of sponsorship jiu-jitsu — the constraint and the idea are inseparable. The reframe of unsold inventory into a victory trophy is the kind of crisis-response move that earns the brand the talkability the original sponsorship was meant to buy.

strategy.rationale: Budweiser is AB InBev's flagship global lager whose World Cup partnership has anchored the brand's mass-sport positioning for four cycles — pivoting that exact sponsorship overnight when the host country's two-day-pre-kickoff alcohol ban broke the contract is on-mission within the brand's own pre-bought platform, not borrowed purpose. The named context is unusually verifiable for a Cannes board because the Qatar alcohol-ban story was front-page global news in November 2022, and the existence of warehoused product is the well-documented constraint that the work pivots on. The strategy weakness is that the case rests on a real PR crisis rather than a deeper consumer insight — the WHY is reactive.

execution.rationale: A single launch tweet pivots a four-year plan in 24 hours, then real-time social, overnight OOH appearing in countries as teams advanced, and a culminating physical beer shipment to Argentina close the loop from announcement to fulfilment. Channel mix is well-suited to the live-tournament rhythm — social for speed, OOH for stature, beer giveaways for participation — and the execution layer compounds week-on-week as more teams qualify, which is the right structural design for a six-week event.

impact.rationale: '25BN impressions' is an entrant-reported reach figure with no audit and no methodology disclosed — a directional signal, not a verified outcome. '$400MM in earned media' is a PR-AVE valuation, the kind of number Cannes Creative Effectiveness juries discount because it monetises impressions rather than business outcomes. '1MN+ beers given away' is concrete inventory disposition but it is also the cost side of the equation, not a Budweiser sales or share outcome. The '#1 World Cup brand' claim and the WSJ/NYT/CBS/CNN/ESPN/Bloomberg coverage are credible signals of earned cultural dominance, but the board does not show the Budweiser business outcome a CFO would care about — global sales, market-share movement, brand-equity lift — which is what would have lifted this into Gold.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the 24-hour pivot is genuine effectiveness craft on a real business crisis, but the impact case is reach + PR-AVE rather than the sales-or-share evidence a Creative Effectiveness Gold demands.

one_liner: The egg becomes the chicken's step counter.
search_doc:
The egg becomes the chicken's step counter.
Mechanisms: product-as-proof, data-as-content, wearable-on-animal, category-claim-reform, transparency-as-differentiator
Idea: Introducing FitChix. Using hen-friendly materials we developed the world's first step counter for a chicken. This device is used to count the steps of our chickens as they roam freely around our farms. Their average daily step count is then printed onto our eggs. So now when people go to the supermarket, they can make a conscious decision about buying eggs from truly free chickens.

extracted.tagline: Changing the egg industry one step at a time.
extracted.context: The egg industry is one of the most misleading food industries in the world. On the supermarket shelf you'll see Free Range, Open Range, Cage Free, Nest Laid, Barn Laid, and Free Run eggs — the list goes on and on. Despite all these wholesome sounding names, a 'Free Range' egg farm can still have up to 10,000 chickens per hectare. At Honest Eggs Co., our regenerative farms only have 30 chickens per hectare. This makes our chickens more free and healthy than any other. That's why it was up to us to change the industry.
extracted.idea: Introducing FitChix. Using hen-friendly materials we developed the world's first step counter for a chicken. This device is used to count the steps of our chickens as they roam freely around our farms. Their average daily step count is then printed onto our eggs. So now when people go to the supermarket, they can make a conscious decision about buying eggs from truly free chickens.
execution: FitChix step-counter device worn by free-range chickens
Step count printed on each individual egg
Redesigned packaging launched in stores
Outdoor and digital media first for eggs
Social-media activations on Instagram, Facebook, TikTok and Strava
Primetime TV news coverage
Multiple prototypes engineered and calibrated to be hen-friendly
press_coverage: "It's not just a great idea, it's a better egg." — Brent Smart, CMO Telstra
"Differentiates in a world of misleading claims and vague labels." — ATTRA

inferred.one_liner: The egg becomes the chicken's step counter.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned the egg shell into a daily fitness-tracker print-out, lifting orders 40%, stockists 222% and online conversation 493% for a regenerative-farm challenger competing against vague 'free range' labels.

visual.style_description: Hero close-up of a single brown egg printed with '21,647 STEPS' in red dot-matrix type, on a soft beige background; left column carries Problem and Idea body copy with a tiny step-counter prototype illustration, right column lists big-percentage results, and a wide horizontal strip of execution photos plus press logos runs along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Strapping fitness trackers to chickens and printing the daily step-count on each egg turns the product itself into the proof of the claim — the egg is the receipt. The mechanic is genuinely original within food packaging and instantly communicable in a single shelf glance.

strategy.rationale: Honest Eggs Co. is a small Australian regenerative-farming challenger brand whose differentiator is a 30-chickens-per-hectare stocking density vs the up-to-10,000-per-hectare standard that 'Free Range' labelling permits — printing per-chicken movement data is exact mission-fit because the product genuinely embodies the claim, not borrowed transparency. The context is unusually verifiable: Australian 'Free Range' labelling can permit 10,000 hens per hectare under the national Model Code, and category-name proliferation (Free Range / Open Range / Cage Free / Nest Laid / Barn Laid / Free Run) is observable on any supermarket shelf. The strategic soft spot is that the brand is small enough that the jury cannot independently size the 'category change' ambition the headline claims.

execution.rationale: The orchestration layers a hen-safe wearable device, prototype engineering, packaging redesign with the printed step-count, OOH and digital, social on Instagram/Facebook/TikTok/Strava (a clever channel-fit because Strava IS a fitness-tracker community), and primetime TV news pickup. Channel choice is sharply matched to the product story — Strava treats the chickens as athletes, retail packaging carries the proof, news amplifies the curiosity — and the multiple prototypes detail signals real product-development investment rather than a one-shot stunt.

impact.rationale: '40% increase in orders' and '222% increase in stockists' are the right business outcomes for a challenger CPG brand, but neither carries an absolute base — '40% of what prior monthly volume?' and '222% of how many starting stockists?' (for a small brand, 222% of 9 stores is 29 stores, which is materially different from 222% of 200 stores). '493% increase in online conversations' is a vanity proxy without baseline. '45,000+ happy chickens' is a deployment scale figure, not a business outcome. The press footprint and Brent Smart (Telstra CMO) quote are credible breadth signals; the jury-grade business case — revenue, market-share, cost-per-acquisition vs incumbents — is not on the board, and the missing absolute bases are exactly the RIGOUR-pattern-1 weakness a Creative Effectiveness Bronze typically reflects.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the product-as-proof idea is genuinely original and the channel-fit is sharp, but the headline-percentage results are all unanchored from absolute bases — exactly the pattern Creative Effectiveness juries discount.

one_liner: Pineapple leaves become the new leather.
search_doc:
Pineapple leaves become the new leather.
Mechanisms: waste-as-product, purpose-driven-product, category-redefinition, industry-partnership-as-distribution
Idea: Every year over 2.5 million tons of pineapple are grown in the Philippines, and for every ton harvested 3 tons of leaves are wasted. Dole partnered with Ananas Anam to turn those discarded pineapple leaves into Pinatex — a vegan, cruelty-free, closed-loop leather alternative used by fashion brands.
Insight: The waste stream of one industry (pineapple farming) is the missing raw material of another (vegan fashion) — a fruit brand can become a textile brand by valorising what it already throws away.

extracted.tagline: Can pineapple leaves be the green alternative to leather?
extracted.context: According to the United Nations Industrial Development Organization, leather is one of the most polluting production sectors on the planet. Could the green answer to leather be found on pineapple leaves?
extracted.idea: Every year over 2.5 million tons of pineapple are grown in the Philippines, and for every ton harvested 3 tons of leaves are wasted. Dole partnered with Ananas Anam to turn those discarded pineapple leaves into Pinatex — a vegan, cruelty-free, closed-loop leather alternative used by fashion brands.
execution: Pinatex sneaker created with Nike using pineapple-leaf leather
Hugo Boss footwear line using Pinatex
H&M 'Happy Pineapple' apparel line in Pinatex
Closed-loop production process recycling residual leaf fibers
metrics: Over 200 brands in more than 80 countries using Pinatex
Commitment to zero waste from Dole's farms by 2025

inferred.insight: The waste stream of one industry (pineapple farming) is the missing raw material of another (vegan fashion) — a fruit brand can become a textile brand by valorising what it already throws away.
inferred.one_liner: Pineapple leaves become the new leather.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a fruit-industry waste stream into a vegan leather adopted by 200+ fashion brands in 80+ countries (Nike, Hugo Boss, H&M), positioning Dole as a sustainability platform on a path to zero-waste farms by 2025.

visual.style_description: Clean editorial grid of close-up product photography: a pineapple crown, sheets of Pinatex material, a sneaker with the Nike swoosh in Pinatex, a Hugo Boss-branded shoe heel and a fashion model in a Pinatex skirt and shirt; bottom band houses Context / Solution / Results columns and a press logo row with the Dole 'Sunshine for All' logo.

idea.rationale: Turning the discarded leaves of a brand's primary crop into a leather alternative is a tidy waste-as-product reframe that lets a fruit company become a textile material supplier. The mechanic is conceptually clean (3 tons of leaf waste per ton of pineapple harvested) and immediately understandable from a single sentence.

strategy.rationale: Dole is one of the world's largest producers of fresh fruit and vegetables, whose 'Sunshine for All' / 'Promise' sustainability platform commits the brand to zero-waste farming — so valorising pineapple-leaf waste into a saleable material is exact mission-fit and a literal embodiment of the zero-waste pledge. The context cites the UN Industrial Development Organization on leather as one of the most polluting production sectors, which is a verifiable third-party data point, and the waste arithmetic (2.5M tons pineapple, 3 tons of leaves per ton harvested in the Philippines) is checkable against agricultural data. The strategic weakness is that Pinatex was actually invented and trademarked by Ananas Anam in 2014 — Dole is a production partner rather than the inventor — so the WHO of the case is partly borrowed credit.

execution.rationale: Distribution is built entirely through industry partnerships rather than paid media: a Nike sneaker, a Hugo Boss footwear line, and H&M's 'Happy Pineapple' apparel collection put the material on the feet and bodies of mass-fashion consumers, with the closed-loop production line as the supply backbone. Channel choice is right for a B2B material story — major fashion partners ARE the channel — but the case as presented is a multi-year industry partnership rollup rather than a designed campaign with discrete phases, which makes the orchestration look slim on a single-page board.

impact.rationale: '200+ brands in more than 80 countries using Pinatex' is the right adoption metric for a B2B material and the most jury-credible figure on the board, but it sums multi-year industry uptake of a product that has existed since 2014 — the campaign-specific incremental contribution is not isolated. 'Commitment to zero waste from Dole's farms by 2025' is a stated commitment, not a delivered result. The board does not show Dole brand-equity lift, consideration-tracker movement, or fresh-fruit sales attribution, and crucially does not isolate which portion of the 200-brand adoption is attributable to Dole's marketing as opposed to Ananas Anam's earlier work — both of which are the standard Creative Effectiveness attribution weaknesses for a long-running platform case.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the waste-as-product platform is a credible long-term brand-purpose story but the case is a multi-year industry rollup with no isolated attribution to Dole's marketing — the kind of unattributed long-tail effectiveness Cannes rewards modestly.

one_liner: Real rock stars confront the office workers stealing their name.
search_doc:
Real rock stars confront the office workers stealing their name.
Mechanisms: celebrity-as-prop, cultural-cliche-subversion, super-bowl-debut, b2b-mass-culture-crossover, name-as-content
Idea: That's not a rock star. Sure that name is overused, often. The official Workday campaign featured a cast of real-life rock legends like Joan Jett, Ozzy Osbourne, Paul Stanley, Gary Clark Jr. and Billy Idol, all confronting corporate workers for calling themselves 'rock stars' in everyday office settings. Across cinema, online, and the digital ecosystem and aired during the Super Bowl, the campaign brought thousands of new customer interest and conversations and ignited a real-life movement. 'Rock Star' was a simple yet strong insight — that office workers are not.
Insight: Office workers love to call themselves 'rock stars'; nothing puts that cliché in its place faster than actual rock legends showing up to take the name back.

extracted.tagline: Rock stars take on the corporate world to reclaim their name.
extracted.context: Workday is a Fortune 500 HR software offering used by some of the world's largest corporations. To establish stronger awareness in their B2B universe and reach decision-makers, 200 billion in annual revenue in 2023, Workday had to break through and convince an audience of senior executives. The brand was given the chance to launch its first Super Bowl spot featuring an ensemble of legendary rockstars, building on the cultural insight that office workers often call themselves 'rock stars'.
extracted.idea: That's not a rock star. Sure that name is overused, often. The official Workday campaign featured a cast of real-life rock legends like Joan Jett, Ozzy Osbourne, Paul Stanley, Gary Clark Jr. and Billy Idol, all confronting corporate workers for calling themselves 'rock stars' in everyday office settings. Across cinema, online, and the digital ecosystem and aired during the Super Bowl, the campaign brought thousands of new customer interest and conversations and ignited a real-life movement. 'Rock Star' was a simple yet strong insight — that office workers are not.
execution: Super Bowl TV spot featuring real rock legends
Cinema, online and digital ecosystem rollout
Social-media activation with the rock-star cast
Confrontational vignettes of musicians scolding office workers
metrics: #1 Most Unexpected Collaboration of the Year (Time)
press_coverage: "Most Unexpected Collaboration of the Year." — TIME

inferred.insight: Office workers love to call themselves 'rock stars'; nothing puts that cliché in its place faster than actual rock legends showing up to take the name back.
inferred.one_liner: Real rock stars confront the office workers stealing their name.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reclaimed the 'rock star' cliché via a Super Bowl ensemble of Joan Jett, Ozzy, Paul Stanley and Billy Idol, driving 4.3B earned impressions, 213% traffic lift and Top B2B Brand on Ad Meter for an HR software brand.

visual.style_description: Cinematic film-still hero of a corporate man and a woman in full KISS face paint sitting at an office meeting table, branded with the yellow 'ROCK STAR' wordmark; a top strip carries portraits of the rock-star cast (Joan Jett, Ozzy, Paul Stanley, Gary Clark Jr., Billy Idol), and the lower half stacks metric callouts in pink/yellow plus a press logo grid and screen-grab thumbnails.

idea.rationale: Recruiting Joan Jett, Ozzy Osbourne, Paul Stanley, Gary Clark Jr. and Billy Idol to scold office workers for stealing the 'rock star' label is a sharp inversion of an over-used office cliché. The mechanic — actual rock legends reclaiming a name from middle managers — is a single readable joke that scales easily across a Super Bowl spot, cinema and social cut-downs.

strategy.rationale: Workday is a Fortune 500 enterprise HR-and-finance SaaS whose business is sold to senior corporate decision-makers — buying a Super Bowl spot to confront an office cliché is mission-adjacent (it targets the right buyer milieu) but the connection between 'rock-star office workers' and Workday's actual HR-management product is thinner than the connection between, say, Dove and self-esteem. The cultural insight that office workers love to call themselves 'rock stars' is plain observable workplace truth but not data-backed on the board, and the named context ('200 billion in annual revenue in 2023') appears to refer to the prospect base rather than Workday's own revenue, which is unclear as written. The strategic risk is that the celebrity ensemble does the carrying and the brand-product link relies on memorability of the gag.

execution.rationale: Super Bowl primary placement, cinema, online and the digital ecosystem give the work mass-cultural visibility that is rare for a B2B SaaS, and the rock-star cast does press and social in their own channels, multiplying the spot through their fan-bases at no marginal cost. The orchestration is essentially one large broadcast moment plus organic celebrity amplification — well-suited to a brand whose buying audience is not retail consumers but senior executives who happen to watch the Super Bowl.

impact.rationale: '213% site traffic increase' is the closest thing to a Workday business outcome but lacks an absolute baseline — 213% of what prior-week sessions? — and a Super Bowl spike will revert. '4.3B earned impressions', '7.9M organic engagements' and '14M social impressions' are entrant-reported reach and engagement vanity metrics, not B2B-business outcomes. 'Top B2B Brand (Super Bowl Admeter)' and 'TIME's #1 Most Unexpected Collaboration' are credible third-party recognitions of the spot itself. The real business outcome a Workday CFO cares about — qualified-lead volume, pipeline-influenced revenue, sales-cycle acceleration, enterprise-deal velocity — is conspicuously absent, which is the standard Creative Effectiveness Bronze pattern for a B2B brand-fame play.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the celebrity ensemble drove undeniable cultural attention but the impact case is reach + traffic spike with no pipeline / deal-velocity / consideration-tracker evidence — the business outcome a B2B Creative Effectiveness Gold would require.

one_liner: Murdered TV characters return to sell life insurance.
search_doc:
Murdered TV characters return to sell life insurance.
Mechanisms: media-hijack, branded-content-integration, fictional-character-resurrection, category-confrontation
Idea: Partners Life teamed up with the country's most popular murder mystery TV show 'The Brokenwood Mysteries' and created a branded content, media-hijacking and product-placement all-in-one campaign in which murdered characters back from the dead in a campaign called 'Last Performance'.
Insight: Underinsurance is a topic people switch off from — but a beloved fictional character coming back to lecture you about it after their on-screen death is impossible to ignore.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE EFFECTIVENESS/BRONZE/The Last Performance - Partners Life (Special, Auckland).webp

extracted.tagline: Bringing TV characters back from the dead to sell life insurance.
extracted.context: When it comes to life assurance, New Zealand is one of the most underinsured in the world. Life insurance provider Partners Life wanted to change that and challenge the nation's mindset to help New Zealanders understand just how important life insurance is.
extracted.idea: Partners Life teamed up with the country's most popular murder mystery TV show 'The Brokenwood Mysteries' and created a branded content, media-hijacking and product-placement all-in-one campaign in which murdered characters back from the dead in a campaign called 'Last Performance'.
execution: Branded content episodes of The Brokenwood Mysteries on TVNZ
Murdered characters resurrected to deliver life-insurance product placement
Cross-platform integration: TV broadcast plus on-demand episode tags
Media campaign appearing in every episode for 24 weeks
press_coverage: Advertising Standards Association complainant: 'I felt I had been tricked into watching this advertisement.'
Stuff: 'A clever example of how brands are using television more creatively.'
TVNZ — Lance Hopkins, Head of Integration: 'A brilliant example of integrating brands around the TV platform.'

inferred.insight: Underinsurance is a topic people switch off from — but a beloved fictional character coming back to lecture you about it after their on-screen death is impossible to ignore.
inferred.one_liner: Murdered TV characters return to sell life insurance.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a beloved New Zealand murder-mystery into a 24-week native-content engine for Partners Life, lifting ad recall to 75% and unprompted brand association by 45%, addressing one of the world's most under-insured populations.

visual.style_description: Cinematic film-still of a shirtless 'corpse' (a murdered character from The Brokenwood Mysteries) lying on a morgue slab with a Y-incision visible, staring at the camera; a top thumbnail strip shows other on-screen deaths and a bottom strip shows the six 'last performance' character cards, with metrics in white inset between blocks of body copy and press-quote columns.

idea.rationale: Bringing the murdered characters of a popular TV drama back from the dead to deliver life-insurance messages collapses the line between programme and ad in a way that is hard to ignore. The mechanic is irreducible to the property (Brokenwood Mysteries), which makes it culturally specific and memorable inside New Zealand.

strategy.rationale: Partners Life is a major New Zealand life-insurance provider whose mission depends on closing the country's well-documented underinsurance gap — using a fictional resurrection to make the consequence of dying without cover tangible is exact mission-fit. The cited context — that New Zealand is one of the most underinsured countries in the world — is plausible (NZ underinsurance has been a known industry problem repeatedly flagged by the Financial Services Council) but the board does not cite a specific gap figure to anchor it. The 'Advertising Standards Association complainant' quote ('I felt I had been tricked into watching this advertisement') is unusually honest jury evidence that the trick worked — and that it crossed a line for some viewers, which the case turns into a feature rather than a defect.

execution.rationale: A 24-week run inside every episode of The Brokenwood Mysteries on TVNZ, plus on-demand integration, is a structurally elegant placement because the campaign and the show literally share airtime — the audience cannot opt out without abandoning the programme. Channel choice is sharp for the target (mass-broadcast viewers of mainstream NZ drama, the demographic most likely to be underinsured) and the seriality compounds: each episode delivers a fresh 'last performance' rather than a single stunt, which is the right architecture for category-confrontation work in an underinsured market.

impact.rationale: '75% recall on Partners Life ads' is a strong brand-fame outcome and the most jury-credible figure on the board, but the surrounding numbers are partially unreadable — '135% [..] above pre-campaign average', '45% [..] mentioned brand', '26% [..]' all have truncated descriptors so a jury cannot confirm what 'pre-campaign average' refers to (sales? quotes? consideration?) or what was mentioned. The board does not show the Partners Life business outcome that matters most — policy quotes, new policy starts, premium income — so the case rests on awareness and recall proxies rather than on closing the underinsurance gap the strategy promised. The truncated metric labels are a Creative Effectiveness penalty in themselves: a jury cannot credit numbers it cannot read.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the media-hijack idea is genuinely original and the channel-fit is exact, but the impact case stops at recall/awareness proxies with partially-illegible labels rather than showing the policy-quote or premium outcome a life-insurance Creative Effectiveness Gold would require.

brand: ITV x CALM (Campaign Against Living Miserably)
one_liner: The last photo of someone who looked completely fine.
search_doc:
The last photo of someone who looked completely fine.
Mechanisms: victim-as-author, cultural-symbol-subversion, documentary-photography-as-campaign, behaviour-change-advocacy
Idea: An outdoor and broadcast campaign built from the genuine last photos of people who later died by suicide, paired with the line 'Suicide doesn't always look suicidal.' to show that warning signs aren't what we expect.
Insight: Suicide doesn't always look suicidal — the last photos taken of people who took their own lives often show them smiling, happy, looking 'fine'.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE EFFECTIVENESS/SILVER/The Last Photo - ITV X CALM (adam&eveDDB, London).webp

extracted.brand: ITV x CALM (Campaign Against Living Miserably)
extracted.context: ITV (the UK's largest commercial broadcaster) and CALM ran a campaign to keep suicide prevention on the national agenda after their long-running 'Project 84' partnership.
extracted.insight: Suicide doesn't always look suicidal — the last photos taken of people who took their own lives often show them smiling, happy, looking 'fine'.
extracted.idea: An outdoor and broadcast campaign built from the genuine last photos of people who later died by suicide, paired with the line 'Suicide doesn't always look suicidal.' to show that warning signs aren't what we expect.
execution: Out-of-home portraits in Soho and other UK locations
Cross-channel ITV broadcast spot
Exhibition of the photos in London's South Bank
Editorial and PR partnership across UK news titles

inferred.one_liner: The last photo of someone who looked completely fine.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Generated 1.5 billion impressions and a 400% year-on-year donation lift while increasing public conversation about suicide by 33% — reframing what suicide 'looks like' at national scale.

visual.style_description: Centerpiece documentary photograph of a smiling young woman walking past a row of OOH portraits of real people who later died by suicide, flanked left by a wall of head-shot portraits of those same people and right by an editorial press-clipping strip; metric figures sit in large white sans-serif beneath the hero.

idea.rationale: Building the campaign from the genuine final photographs of real people who died by suicide — paired with a single, counter-intuitive line — is a strong subversion of the visual cliché of what a 'suicidal person' looks like. The work is irreducible: you cannot do this campaign without the actual last photos, which is what makes it both honest and unrepeatable.

strategy.rationale: CALM (Campaign Against Living Miserably) is the UK's largest male-suicide-prevention charity and ITV is the UK's largest commercial broadcaster, whose multi-year partnership (Project 84, Britain Get Talking) has anchored mainstream-media advocacy for mental health — so this work is exact mission-fit for both partners. The insight that suicide doesn't always look suicidal — that the last photographs often show people smiling and 'fine' — is the campaign's epistemological core and is corroborated by the chosen photographs themselves rather than asserted from a third-party study. The strategy is strongest where it names a real cultural misperception (warning signs aren't what we expect) and weakest where the board does not cite a UK suicide-statistics anchor (e.g. CALM's known 'leading killer of men under 50' line) to size the problem.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs Soho OOH portraits, an ITV broadcast spot, a South Bank exhibition and a UK news-title partnership — four channels that converge on physical and editorial inhabitations of British public space. The choice of OOH portraits in dense urban areas and a curated exhibition are well-matched to a topic that needs space and stillness, and the broadcast spot via ITV's own air guarantees reach without paid-media outlay, which is structurally elegant.

impact.rationale: The '400% increase in donations (year on year)' is exactly the right business outcome for a charity case and the most jury-credible number on the board, though the absolute base — '400% of what prior-year baseline?' — is not disclosed, so the strength of the lift depends on the prior baseline (a small charity's 400% lift is materially different to a large one's). The '33% increase in conversations mentioning suicide' is a behaviour-change proxy that is the right kind of metric for prevention work; '1.5 billion media impressions' is entrant-reported and unaudited. '500,000+ exhibition visitors' is a concrete physical-attendance figure that is harder to inflate, and 750+ pieces of news coverage is a credible breadth-of-press signal.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the donation-lift and conversation-share numbers are the right behaviour-change outcomes for a charity case, but the absence of an absolute donation base — and no longitudinal follow-through beyond the launch year — keeps it from the Gold band.

one_liner: Deepfake moms speak the toxic beauty advice TikTok already gives.
search_doc:
Deepfake moms speak the toxic beauty advice TikTok already gives.
Mechanisms: deepfake-as-message, trusted-voice-hijack, social-experiment-film, platform-criticism, self-esteem-advocacy
Idea: We put the same toxic advice girls hear every day into the mouth of the person they trust the most: their mom. Using cutting-edge face-mapping and voice-cloning technology, we deepfaked real moms to give this toxic advice to their daughters in a groundbreaking social experiment. The short film quickly went global, garnering 13.6 million organic views and 1.7 billion impressions in the first ten days. Launched late April, the campaign is on track to become the most successful in Dove history.

extracted.tagline: Toxic influencers, moms and deepfake technology. Combined in a one-of-a-kind experiment.
extracted.context: Toxic beauty advice is causing a self-esteem crisis in young girls. It has been linked to elevated rates of depression and a rise in teenage suicides. And it's only getting worse. Social media algorithms are feeding girls harmful beauty hacks, dangerous trends and unwanted products as soon as they get their first phone. Tragically, this toxic advice has become as common that girls don't even realize it's toxic. It was time for Dove to act.
extracted.idea: We put the same toxic advice girls hear every day into the mouth of the person they trust the most: their mom. Using cutting-edge face-mapping and voice-cloning technology, we deepfaked real moms to give this toxic advice to their daughters in a groundbreaking social experiment. The short film quickly went global, garnering 13.6 million organic views and 1.7 billion impressions in the first ten days. Launched late April, the campaign is on track to become the most successful in Dove history.
execution: Deepfake face-mapping and voice-cloning of mothers
Short-film social experiment with real mother-daughter pairs
Global PR rollout in 60+ countries
Coverage on national TV news
Earned media across 300+ outlets

inferred.one_liner: Deepfake moms speak the toxic beauty advice TikTok already gives.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Used deepfake voice-cloning of mothers to make algorithmic beauty advice unignorable to daughters, generating 1.7B earned impressions, 13.6M organic views and 99% positive sentiment in the first 10 days across 60+ countries.

visual.style_description: Cinematic film-still hero of a mother on a blue-lit talk-show set with the words 'Toxic Influence' rendered as a transparent overlay across her face, suggesting the deepfake mask; top strip stacks TV-news screen grabs, left column carries Problem and Idea body copy, right column lists big-number results, and a press logo strip closes the bottom.

idea.rationale: Putting toxic algorithmic beauty advice into the mouth of the person girls trust most — their own mother — via deepfake is a sharp social-experiment construction that makes harmful content unignorable through its source. The technical means (face-mapping plus voice-cloning) is also the message: if you can fake mom, you can fake anyone, including the trends girls believe.

strategy.rationale: Dove is Unilever's global personal-care brand whose long-running Self-Esteem Project has anchored beauty-confidence advocacy for over two decades, so naming the algorithmic-feed harm to young girls is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed cause. The named context links toxic beauty advice to 'elevated rates of depression and a rise in teenage suicides' — a strong claim that is plausible against published adolescent-mental-health literature but is not anchored on the board with a specific source. The plain observable fact — that social-media algorithms feed beauty-hack content to girls from their first phone — is the kind of cultural truth a jury can corroborate from their own platform experience.

execution.rationale: A single short-film social experiment with real mother-daughter pairs anchors the work, with deepfake production as the production craft, and a global PR rollout across 60+ countries and 300+ outlets plus national TV news pickup multiplies the film into earned coverage. Channel choice is right for an awareness piece on platform-algorithm harm — the message lives where the harm lives (social) and is amplified into news/talk-show airtime where parents are the audience. The orchestration is essentially one hero asset plus PR distribution, which is a slimmer architecture than a multi-layer campaign.

impact.rationale: '13.6 million organic views' and '1.7 billion earned media impressions' are entrant-reported reach figures with no audit and no absolute base — directional rather than business-grade. '99% positive sentiment on social' is a percentage without a stated post-volume or methodology — RIGOUR pattern 1 directly applies. '300+ media outlets in 60+ countries' is a credible breadth-of-press signal. The deeper gap is that the board does not show a Dove business outcome — Self-Esteem Project enrolment, parent-conversation lift, brand-equity tracker movement, sales or share — for a campaign explicitly described as 'on track to become the most successful in Dove history', which is a strong claim that the metrics on the board do not substantiate. For a Creative Effectiveness Lion, the absence of a Dove-specific behaviour or equity outcome is the load-bearing weakness.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the deepfake mechanic is genuinely original and on-mission for Dove, but the impact case is reach + sentiment without an absolute base and without a Dove-business outcome — the exact failure pattern Creative Effectiveness Bronze typically reflects.

one_liner: The World Cup trophy becomes a PedidosYa delivery.
search_doc:
The World Cup trophy becomes a PedidosYa delivery.
Mechanisms: platform-jailbreak, push-notification-hijack, real-time-tracking-as-content, cultural-moment-hijack
Idea: Use PedidosYa, Argentina's biggest delivery app, to let users follow the World Cup trophy as it travels through a map — knowing exactly where it is at any given time.
Insight: When Argentina won the World Cup after 36 years, every Argentinian who had the app received a push notification that said 'Your order is on the way. Follow it here.' Everyone thought they had got scammed, but no.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE EFFECTIVENESS/GOLD/World Cup Delivery - Pedidosya (Gut Buenos Aires).jpg

extracted.insight: When Argentina won the World Cup after 36 years, every Argentinian who had the app received a push notification that said 'Your order is on the way. Follow it here.' Everyone thought they had got scammed, but no.
extracted.idea: Use PedidosYa, Argentina's biggest delivery app, to let users follow the World Cup trophy as it travels through a map — knowing exactly where it is at any given time.
execution: Push notification sent to app users saying 'Your order is on the way'
In-app live map tracking the World Cup trophy's journey
Trending hashtag activations: #PedidosYa, #ArgentinaCampeon, #MessiCampeon
'Your order is on the way' tap-to-follow real-time module
Zero paid media — earned distribution only

inferred.context: Argentina had not won a FIFA World Cup in 36 years, and when victory came in 2022 the country was looking for any extension of the celebration.
inferred.one_liner: The World Cup trophy becomes a PedidosYa delivery.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned the delivery app's tracker into a national celebration tool for Argentina's World Cup win, generating +32% brand mentions and +26 million reach with zero paid media spend.

visual.style_description: Hero shot of the golden FIFA World Cup trophy with a small plane circling it like a globe against a blue sky, evoking the trophy's flight to Buenos Aires; large white 'World Cup delivery' wordmark top-left, with context and idea text columns flanking the trophy and a results strip of app-screen cards at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Repurposing the food-delivery push notification — the single most familiar UI moment in an Argentinian's day — to track the World Cup trophy is a clean platform-native reframe that nobody else with a tracker app thought to make. The 'Your order is on the way' UX joke is instantly readable as both prank and patriotic gesture, which is why it spread.

strategy.rationale: PedidosYa is Argentina's home-grown delivery leader (part of Delivery Hero), whose product equity is built on real-time tracking — so converting that tracker into a national-celebration tool is a coherent extension of what users already trust the app to do, not borrowed purpose. The insight is the 36-year wait for an Argentine World Cup win and the fact that every fan was looking for any extension of the celebration, which is plain observable cultural fact. The board does not anchor 'biggest delivery app' with share data, but PedidosYa's category leadership in Argentina is not in dispute.

execution.rationale: A single push notification to the installed base is the launch act, the in-app live map is the experience, and three trending hashtags (#PedidosYa, #ArgentinaCampeon, #MessiCampeon) ride the national conversation — the orchestration is tight because each layer reinforces the same platform-native joke. Channel choice is on-target: the audience is already in the app, the moment is the most-watched sports event in Argentine history, and the activation is delivered through earned distribution with zero paid media.

impact.rationale: The '+32% mentions' and '+26 million reach' figures are percentages and reach numbers without an absolute base or a control window — '+32% of what baseline, measured over what window?' is not disclosed on the board, so the strength of the lift is directional. The '$0 in media investment' is the most jury-credible claim because it implies a cost-per-outcome story for a category where competitors spent heavily on World Cup buys. The board does not show the business outcome a delivery app would care about — order volume, MAU lift, app-install spikes during or after the moment — so the case rests on earned-attention proxies rather than commerce.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because the platform-jailbreak idea is exact-fit for what PedidosYa's tracker already does, the zero-paid-media efficiency is the standout effectiveness story, and the absence of order-volume data is the main thing keeping it out of the highest band.

one_liner: Your year in music becomes a shareable personality.
search_doc:
Your year in music becomes a shareable personality.
Mechanisms: data-as-content, personalisation-as-ritual, platform-native-experience, shareability-by-design
Idea: Wrapped provided each of our 456 million users with a personalised recap of how they listened in 2022, celebrating our listeners for exactly who they are.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE EFFECTIVENESS/BRONZE/Wrapped On Platform Experience - Spotify (Spotify, New York).webp

extracted.context: In its eighth year on the cultural calendar, 2022 Wrapped put on its biggest show ever — giving listeners ways to visualise, discuss and share their annual Spotify recap like never before. Available in 111+ markets, the in-app experience culminated in a worldwide celebration of unique listeners and self expression, reflective of a year of emergence and experiments as we got to know ourselves again.
extracted.idea: Wrapped provided each of our 456 million users with a personalised recap of how they listened in 2022, celebrating our listeners for exactly who they are.
execution: In-app experience playfully highlighted each user's individuality and quirks
Shared everyone's top genres in a trip to the genre-verse
Broke down daily listening habits from sunrise to sunset
Revealed users' top songs, podcasts and artists
Created 16 unique 'Listening Personalities' matched to users based on listening data

inferred.one_liner: Your year in music becomes a shareable personality.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned 456M users' listening data into 156M+ engaged sessions and 52.5B+ TikTok views, ranking as the 14th most-watched event in human history and establishing Wrapped as an annual cultural moment.

visual.style_description: Maximalist neon-pop layout: lime-green panel left with the bold 'Wrapped' wordmark and floral motif over a results column, centre and right show a grid of phone mockups featuring Spotify Wrapped screens — 'Mellow City Pop Mornings,' 'Feel Good Indie Afternoons,' 'Your Top Artists,' a sun-like 'Devotee' personality and shareable artist cards — set against a dense magenta-and-yellow background.

idea.rationale: Returning the platform's own usage data to each of 456 million users as a personalised, shareable 'personality' is an annual cultural mechanic Spotify itself built — the 2022 'Listening Personalities' iteration adds 16 named character archetypes and a genre-verse trip on top of the established Wrapped template. The novelty is incremental rather than reinventive; the strength is the ritual it has become.

strategy.rationale: Spotify is the world's largest music-streaming platform, whose mission is to 'unlock the potential of human creativity' by connecting fans to artists — handing users a year-end personalised data portrait reinforces that platform-as-discovery positioning, so mission-fit is genuine. The named context — 2022 was Wrapped's eighth year, available in 111+ markets, with 456M users — is verifiable Spotify reporting (the 456M MAU figure aligns with Q4 2022 earnings). The strategic weakness is that the case as presented is essentially 'we ran our annual product feature again' — there is no documented behavioural insight or strategic shift specific to 2022 that would distinguish this year's Wrapped from any other, so the WHY is platform-business-as-usual rather than a fresh strategic move.

execution.rationale: The execution is platform-native by design: an in-app experience that segments users into 16 'Listening Personalities', visualises daily listening from sunrise to sunset, and produces share-cards optimised for off-platform spread to TikTok, Instagram and Twitter. Channel-fit is exact because the experience IS the channel and the share-cards make every user a media buyer; the 16-personality system is a meaningful orchestration upgrade on previous Wrapped iterations because it gives users a discrete identity to claim rather than a list.

impact.rationale: '156M+ engaged users' is meaningful platform-engagement scale, but it is also 34% of the 456M user base — engagement-rate calculated against an available base, not a campaign-attributable lift over a control. '6.5M+ social mentions', '66.5M+ shares' and '52.5B+ TikTok views' are off-platform virality signals that are entrant-aggregated and unaudited. '#1 trending on TikTok and Twitter' and '14th most-watched event in human history' are talkability claims; the latter conflates 'event' categories in a way no jury can verify. The real Spotify business outcome — Premium-conversion lift, churn reduction, paid-subscriber adds during the Wrapped window — is what a Creative Effectiveness jury would weight heavily and is conspicuously absent from the board, especially given Spotify's well-known subscriber-economics narrative. The native-to-category check therefore fails: vast vanity engagement, no business-grade KPI.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because Wrapped is a genuine annual cultural ritual but the case substitutes off-platform reach for the subscriber-conversion or churn evidence a streaming-business Creative Effectiveness Gold demands.
